title: Kundalini and Grief: The Losses That Awakening Forces You to Face

body:
Kundalini and Grief: The Losses That Awakening Forces You to Face

Hey. You're probably reading this because you're in it, aren't you? That feeling like your entire world is shattering, like something's been ripped away, and you're left with this hollow, aching space. It's 3 AM, and you can't sleep, can you? Your mind's racing, your body's buzzing, or maybe it's just a cold, heavy dread. I know that feeling. I've been there. I'm here to tell you that what you're experiencing - this grief, this deep sense of loss - it's a real, often brutal, part of the kundalini process. It's not just in your head. It's not a sign you're broken. It's part of the shedding, the burning away of everything you thought you were.

When kundalini wakes up, it doesn't ask for permission. It just goes. And what it does is dismantle. It takes apart your carefully constructed reality, piece by piece. And guess what? When things get taken apart, you lose them. You lose parts of yourself, parts of your life, parts of your understanding of how the world works. And that, my friend, is grief. It's a natural, unavoidable response to loss. You're not losing a person in the traditional sense, though sometimes that happens too. You're losing something far more fundamental: your old self.

I remember lying awake for nights, feeling like I was mourning someone I couldn't name. It was me, but not me. It was the me I thought I was supposed to be, the me I'd spent a lifetime building. And kundalini just came in and bulldozed it all. It's terrifying, honestly. You feel so alone, like no one could possibly understand. But here's the thing: many of us do. This path isn't all bliss and light; it's often a dark, messy, agonizing journey through the wreckage of your own making.

The Death of the Ego and the Self You Knew

Look, when kundalini activates, it's not just a spiritual experience. It's a biological, psychological, and existential earthquake. It starts re-wiring your nervous system, your brain, your very perception of reality. And the first casualty? Your ego. Not ego in the sense of being arrogant, but ego in the sense of that carefully crafted identity you've been carrying around your whole life. The stories you tell yourself about who you are, what you like, what you believe, what you're capable of - all of it gets challenged, scrutinized, and often, dissolved.

You might find yourself questioning everything. Your career, your relationships, your hobbies, your political views, your spiritual beliefs - everything can suddenly feel hollow or wrong. This isn't just a phase; it's a deep, internal re-evaluation that can feel like a complete betrayal of your past self. You're losing the comfort of certainty, the familiarity of your old ways of thinking and being. That's a huge loss. It's like your internal compass has been spun wildly, and you no longer know north from south. You don't recognize yourself in the mirror sometimes, do you? You look at your life and wonder, "Who is this person? What am I even doing?"

This isn't just a metaphor. Your brain literally changes. Your neural pathways get reconfigured. Old patterns of thought and emotion, once deeply ingrained, begin to break down. And that breakdown, while ultimately freeing, feels like a death. It's the death of the familiar, the predictable, the "safe" you. You're grieving the loss of that old identity, even if it was limiting, even if it was causing you pain. Because at least you knew it. At least it was yours. Now, you're in a liminal space, an in-between, and it's disorienting as hell. You're losing the sense of control you thought you had over your life, over your mind. That's a massive loss, and it calls for massive grief. The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link) really gets into the physical and psychological changes that can feel so overwhelming. It helped me understand I wasn't just losing my mind.

The Loss of Old Relationships and Social Structures

And it's not just your internal world that gets shaken up. Your external world often does too. When you change so at its core, the people around you might not understand. They might not like the new you. Or rather, they might not like the you who's shedding the old skin. You're not fitting into the old boxes anymore, are you? Your interests shift, your values might re-align, your tolerance for superficiality might plummet. Suddenly, conversations that used to be easy become impossible. Friendships that felt solid start to fray. Family dynamics become strained because you're no longer playing the role they expect you to play.

This is incredibly painful. You're losing connections, losing your place in certain social circles. You might feel like an alien, like you've been dropped onto a different planet where everyone speaks a language you no longer understand. This isn't just about people leaving you; sometimes, you're the one who has to pull away because those old connections no longer serve your evolving self. And that's a grief too, a very real, tangible grief for the relationships that once were, for the comfort of belonging. You're losing your tribe, or at least, your old tribe. It leaves you feeling isolated, doesn't it? Like you're walking this path entirely alone. And honestly, for a while, you kind of are. But here's the kicker - that isolation forces you inwards, forces you to find strength you never knew you had.

The Disillusionment with Old Belief Systems

What about all those beliefs you held dear? Your spiritual path, your political affiliations, your understanding of right and wrong? Kundalini often shatters them. It exposes the cracks, the inconsistencies, the dogmas that kept you bound. You might have been a devout follower of a particular religion, only to find that its teachings now feel hollow or even oppressive. Or you might have been an atheist, and now you're having experiences that defy all logic. This can be deeply unsettling. You're losing your intellectual and spiritual safety nets. You're losing the comfort of having a clear-cut worldview. That's a huge loss of certainty, a loss of the very foundations upon which you built your understanding of the universe. It's like the rug gets pulled out from under you, and you're left free-falling through existential questions without any easy answers. And that free-fall? It's terrifying, and it brings with it a grief for the simplicity you once had. When Spirit Leaps: Navigating the Process of Spiritual Awakening by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) talks a lot about this kind of disillusionment and how to cope when your old world crumbles.

The Loss of Your Sense of Normalcy and Safety

Remember what "normal" felt like? Remember feeling safe in your own skin, in your own home, in your own mind? Kundalini often takes that away, at least for a while. Your body might be doing weird things - vibrations, heat, involuntary movements, strange pains, intense energy surges. Your mind might be racing, or you might be experiencing intense visions, sounds, or emotions that feel completely alien. You might be having what some call "psychic" experiences, seeing or feeling things you never did before. This can make you feel like you're losing your mind, like you're going crazy. And that fear, that terror of losing your sanity, is a deep loss of your sense of safety and normalcy. You're grieving the predictability of your own body and mind.

You might suddenly be hypersensitive to sounds, lights, other people's energies. Crowds become unbearable. Loud noises feel like physical assaults. You might find yourself withdrawing, needing more solitude than ever before, just to cope with the sheer intensity of your internal experience. This withdrawal, while necessary for your integration, contributes to that feeling of isolation and loss. You're losing the ability to function in the world the way you once did. You're losing the ease of just "being." Everything feels harder, more intense, more overwhelming. This isn't weakness; it's a response to an immense internal transformation. You're literally re-calibrating. And that recalibration period, that feeling of being unmoored, is a source of deep, deep grief. You're longing for the days when things felt simple, when you didn't have to constantly manage this internal inferno. I spent months feeling like I was walking around with no skin, raw and exposed to everything. It was a brutal loss of my old resilience.

Processing the Grief: What You Can Do

So, what do you do with all this grief? You feel it. You really, truly feel it. You don't push it down, you don't intellectualize it away. You let it wash over you. It's messy, it's uncomfortable, it's painful. But it's necessary. Here's what I learned, what helped me crawl through those dark nights:

  • Acknowledge the Loss: Don't minimize what you're going through. You're losing a lot. Call it what it is: grief. Giving it a name helps.
  • Allow Yourself to Grieve: Cry. Scream into a pillow. Write in a journal. Talk to someone who gets it - if you can find them. Don't judge your emotions. They're valid.
  • Seek Support (Carefully): This is tough because many people won't understand. But try to find a therapist who's open to spiritual experiences, or a support group for spiritual emergence. Sometimes just knowing you're not alone is everything. The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) is fantastic for finding resources and understanding what's happening.
  • Practice Radical Self-Care: This isn't optional. It's survival. Rest. Eat nourishing food. Spend time in nature. Meditate (if it feels right, sometimes it's too intense). Do whatever brings you a tiny bit of comfort or peace, even if it's just watching a silly movie. You're in a highly vulnerable state. Treat yourself like you're recovering from a serious illness, because in a way, you are.
  • Understand the Cycle: Know that this isn't forever. Grief comes in waves. There will be moments of intense pain, and then moments of calm, even glimpses of peace. It's not linear. You're not failing if you feel good one day and terrible the next. That's just how grief works.
  • Trust the Process (Eventually): This is the hardest one when you're in the thick of it. But know that kundalini isn't trying to destroy you; it's trying to purify you. It's clearing out everything that's not truly you, everything that's holding you back from your true potential. The losses are making space for something new, something more authentic, more expansive. It doesn't feel like that now, I know. But it's true.

The Phoenix Rising: What Comes After the Ashes

I know it feels like nothing but ashes right now. But after the burning, after the grief, after the letting go, something new starts to emerge. You don't just get back what you lost. You get something entirely different, something far more real and resilient. You become more compassionate, not just to others, but to yourself. You develop a deep inner knowing, a wisdom that comes only from having walked through the fire. Your intuition sharpens. You start living from a place of authenticity, not from a place of expectation or fear.

You'll find new connections, people who truly see and understand the new you. You'll discover a deeper purpose, a clearer sense of direction. The grief doesn't disappear entirely; it becomes a part of your story, a scar that reminds you of how far you've come. But it no longer defines you. You learn to carry it, to integrate it, and to use it as fuel for your continued growth.

This isn't some fluffy, "everything's going to be okay" platitude. This is hard-won truth. I've been through it. I've seen others go through it. It's the ultimate dark night of the soul, and it's not for the faint of heart. But you're not faint of heart, are you? You're here, you're reading this, you're trying to understand. That's strength right there. You're not crazy. You're not alone. You're in the midst of a deep transformation, and grief is simply one of its most challenging, yet ultimately necessary, companions. The Stormy Search for the Self by Stanislav & Christina Grof (paid link) explores how these intense experiences, even the terrifying ones, can lead to incredible growth. It's a tough read, but it's validating.

So yeah, let yourself grieve. Let the tears come. Let the anger out. Let the confusion be. You're shedding layers, and that process is painful. But underneath it all, you're discovering something truly magnificent. You're discovering who you truly are, beyond all the roles and stories. And that, my friend, is worth every tear, every moment of darkness. Hang in there. You're stronger than you know.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
